column3: Chapter XII - Determination of Tax in Certain Special Cases
column5: New Tax Regime Slabs (AY 2024-25 and FY 2025-26)
column6: The income-tax payable in respect of the total income of a person, being an individual or HUF, shall, at his option or by default, be computed at concessional rates.
column7: Section 115BAC details the New Tax Regime, which is the default regime from AY 2024-25. Slabs for FY 2025-26 (AY 2026-27): Up to ₹4,00,000: Nil; ₹4,00,001 to ₹8,00,000: 5%; ₹8,00,001 to ₹12,00,000: 10%; ₹12,00,001 to ₹16,00,000: 15%; ₹16,00,001 to ₹20,00,000: 20%; ₹20,00,001 to ₹24,00,000: 25%; Above ₹24,00,000: 30%. Standard deduction is ₹75,000. Rebate S.87A makes tax nil up to ₹7 Lakhs (plus standard deduction). Most deductions (80C, 80D, S.24) are not allowed under this regime.
column8: Income Tax Slabs, New Tax Regime, Section 115BAC, Standard Deduction, Tax Slabs 2025, S.115BAC
column20: Not Applicable / N.A.
column21: Taxpayer must opt-out to choose the old regime.

column3: Chapter I - Charge of Income-tax
column5: Old Tax Regime Slabs and Rates
column6: Income-tax shall be charged at the rates specified in the Finance Act in respect of the total income of the previous year of every person.
column7: The Old Tax Regime slabs (which allow all standard exemptions and deductions): Up to ₹2,50,000: Nil; ₹2,50,001 to ₹5,00,000: 5% (fully rebated under S.87A up to 5L); ₹5,00,001 to ₹10,00,000: 20%; Above ₹10,00,000: 30%. Taxpayers can avail deductions under Section 80C (PPF, LIC, etc.), Section 80D (health insurance), Section 24(b) (home loan interest), and HRA exemption.
column8: Old Tax Regime Slabs, Old Tax slabs, Section 80C deductions, HRA exemption slabs, old vs new regime
column20: Not Applicable / N.A.
column21: Taxpayers must actively opt into the Old Regime during filing; otherwise New Regime applies by default.

column3: Chapter VI-A - Deductions in Respect of Certain Incomes
column5: Section 80C Deductions (1.5 Lakh Limit)
column6: In computing the total income of an assessee, there shall be deducted, in accordance with and subject to the provisions of this section, any sums paid or deposited in the previous year by the assessee.
column7: Section 80C provides a tax deduction of up to ₹1,50,000 per year from gross total income. Allowed investments: Public Provident Fund (PPF), Employee Provident Fund (EPF), Equity Linked Savings Schemes (ELSS), Life Insurance Premium, National Savings Certificate (NSC), Principal repayment of Home Loan, Tuition Fees for up to 2 children, Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana (SSY), and 5-year tax-saving fixed deposits. Only available in the Old Tax Regime.
column8: Section 80C, PPF, ELSS, Tax Saving, Life Insurance, Home Loan Principal, S.80C, Deductions
column9: CIT v. Rajasthan State Electricity Board
column11: Deduction is allowable on actual payment basis during the relevant financial year.
column20: Maximum ₹1,50,000 per financial year
column21: Not available to taxpayers under the New Tax Regime S.115BAC.

column3: Chapter VI-A - Deductions in Respect of Certain Incomes
column5: Medical Insurance Premium Deduction
column6: In computing the total income of an assessee, there shall be deducted sums paid to effect or keep in force an insurance on the health of the assessee or his family or parents.
column7: Section 80D allows a deduction for health insurance premium and preventive health check-up. 1. Self, Spouse, and Dependent Children: Limit is ₹25,000 (increases to ₹50,000 if self/spouse is a senior citizen). 2. Parents: Additional limit of ₹25,000 (increases to ₹50,000 if parent is a senior citizen). Max deduction can be ₹1,00,000 (both self and parents are senior citizens). Preventive health check-up is capped at ₹5,000 (within the overall limit). Payment must be made by any mode other than cash (cash is allowed only for preventive health check-ups). Only available in the Old Tax Regime.
column8: Section 80D, Health Insurance, Medical Claim, Senior Citizen health, S.80D, Deductions
column20: Up to ₹25,000 / ₹50,000 / ₹1,00,000 depending on senior citizen status
column21: Insurance premium payments must not be made in cash; cash is permitted only for preventive health check-ups up to ₹5,000.

column3: Chapter IV - Computation of Total Income
column5: Deduction for Home Loan Interest
column6: Where the property has been acquired, constructed, repaired, renewed or reconstructed with borrowed capital, the amount of any interest payable on such capital shall be deducted.
column7: Section 24(b) allows deduction of interest paid on a home loan. For a self-occupied property, the maximum deduction is ₹2,00,000 per financial year. Construction or purchase must be completed within 5 years from the end of the FY in which capital was borrowed; otherwise, the limit is reduced to ₹30,000. For let-out properties, there is no limit on interest deduction, but loss from house property that can be offset against other income is capped at ₹2,00,000. Only available in the Old Tax Regime.
column8: Section 24, Home Loan Interest, Loss from House Property, Self-occupied property interest, S.24(b)
column9: CIT v. Podar Cement Pvt. Ltd.
column11: Established that for tax purposes, the owner of a house property is the person who receives the income, even if registration formalities are incomplete.
column20: Maximum ₹2,00,000 for self-occupied property
column21: Reduced to ₹30,000 if construction is not completed within 5 years from loan year.

column3: Chapter IV - Profits and Gains of Business or Profession
column5: Presumptive Taxation for Businesses
column6: Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in sections 28 to 43C, in the case of an eligible assessee engaged in an eligible business, a sum equal to eight per cent of the total turnover or gross receipts shall be deemed to be the profits.
column7: Section 44AD offers a simplified presumptive tax scheme for small businesses. Income is estimated at 8% of total turnover/gross receipts (reduced to 6% for receipts via digital transactions like UPI, net banking, cards). The turnover limit to opt for this scheme is ₹3 Crore (if cash transactions are under 5% of gross receipts; otherwise ₹2 Crore). Taxpayers under this scheme do not need to maintain detailed books of accounts or get an audit.
column8: Section 44AD, Presumptive Taxation, Small Business, Books of Accounts Audit, S.44AD, 6 percent, 8 percent
column9: CIT v. Reliance Petroproducts Pvt. Ltd.
column11: Determined that making a claim that is not accepted by the Assessing Officer does not automatically attract penalty under S.271(1)(c) for inaccurate particulars.
column19: Profits and Gains of Business or Profession
column20: 6% on digital turnover and 8% on cash turnover
column21: Only available to resident individuals, HUFs, and partnership firms (excluding LLP).

column3: Chapter IV - Profits and Gains of Business or Profession
column5: Presumptive Taxation for Professionals
column6: In the case of an assessee, being a resident in India, who is engaged in a profession referred to in sub-section (1) of section 44AA, a sum equal to fifty per cent of the total gross receipts shall be deemed to be the profits.
column7: Section 44ADA offers a presumptive tax scheme for professionals (doctors, engineers, CAs, lawyers, architects, IT professionals). The gross receipts limit is ₹75 Lakhs (if cash receipts are under 5% of total; otherwise ₹50 Lakhs). Profits are presumed to be 50% of gross receipts, and the professional pays tax on this amount. No need to maintain books of accounts or undergo tax audit.
column8: Section 44ADA, Presumptive Professional, Doctors Engineers Lawyers CAs tax, S.44ADA
column19: Profits and Gains of Business or Profession
column20: 50% of gross receipts deemed as taxable income
column21: Only applicable to specific professions listed under Section 44AA(1).

column3: Chapter XII - Determination of Tax in Certain Special Cases
column5: LTCG Tax on Equity and Mutual Funds
column6: The tax payable by an assessee on long-term capital gains exceeding one lakh twenty-five thousand rupees from transfer of equity shares, equity oriented mutual funds, or business trust units shall be calculated at twelve and a half per cent.
column7: Section 112A governs Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG) tax on equity shares and equity-oriented mutual funds (held for more than 12 months). Effective from July 2024, gains exceeding ₹1.25 Lakhs in a financial year are taxed at 12.5% (previously 10% above ₹1 Lakh). No indexation benefit is allowed for these assets under S.112A.
column8: Section 112A, LTCG, Long Term Capital Gains, Shares Mutual Funds, 12.5 percent, S.112A
column9: CIT v. Vodafone International Holdings BV
column11: A landmark judgment on taxation of capital gains in offshore transactions, establishing boundaries of tax planning vs tax evasion.
column20: 12.5% on gains exceeding ₹1.25 Lakhs
column21: STT (Securities Transaction Tax) must have been paid on purchase and sale of shares.

column3: Chapter XII - Determination of Tax in Certain Special Cases
column5: STCG Tax on Equity and Mutual Funds
column6: The tax payable by an assessee on short-term capital gains from transfer of equity shares, equity oriented mutual funds, or business trust units shall be calculated at twenty per cent.
column7: Section 111A governs Short-Term Capital Gains (STCG) tax on equity shares and equity-oriented mutual funds (held for 12 months or less). Effective from July 2024, these gains are taxed at 20% (previously 15%). The transaction must be subject to Securities Transaction Tax (STT).
column8: Section 111A, STCG, Short Term Capital Gains, Shares Mutual Funds, 20 percent, S.111A
column20: 20% flat rate
column21: STT must have been paid at the time of sale.

column3: Chapter II - Incomes which do not form part of Total Income
column5: House Rent Allowance (HRA) Exemption
column6: Any special allowance specifically granted to an assessee by his employer to meet expenditure actually incurred on payment of rent in respect of residential accommodation occupied by him is exempt.
column7: Section 10(13A) governs the tax exemption on House Rent Allowance (HRA) for salaried employees. The exempt amount is the minimum of three values: 1. Actual HRA received; 2. Rent paid minus 10% of basic salary; 3. 50% of basic salary (for metro cities: Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai) or 40% of basic salary (for non-metro cities). Taxpayer must live in rented accommodation and pay rent. Only available under the Old Tax Regime.
column8: Section 10(13A), HRA Exemption, House Rent Allowance, Rent receipts, HRA Calculator, S.10(13A)
column20: Minimum of the three specified conditions
column21: Not available to taxpayers under the New Tax Regime S.115BAC.
column22: Section 80GG (Rent deduction when HRA not received)

column3: Chapter IV - Profits and Gains of Business or Profession
column5: General Business Expenses Deduction
column6: Any expenditure (not being expenditure of the character described in sections 30 to 36 and not being in the nature of capital expenditure or personal expenses of the assessee), laid out or expended wholly and exclusively for the purposes of the business or profession shall be allowed.
column7: Section 37(1) is the general section that allows deduction of any business expense not covered under other specific sections (S.30-36). The expense must be: 1. Revenue in nature (not capital); 2. Wholly and exclusively for the business; 3. Not personal in nature; 4. Not for any purpose that is an offense or prohibited by law. Common examples include salary to staff, office rent, electricity, printing, advertising, traveling, and communication costs.
column8: Section 37, Business expenses deduction, office rent utility, salary to staff tax deduction, revenue expenditure, S.37(1)
column11: The expression 'for the purpose of the business' is wider than 'for the purpose of earning profits'. It covers administration, preservation, and protection of business.
column12: CIT v. Reliance Petroproducts Pvt. Ltd.
column14: Simply making an incorrect claim of deduction under S.37 does not amount to providing inaccurate particulars or attract concealment penalty.
column20: Allowed fully if expenses are genuine and exclusively for business
column21: Expenditure incurred on CSR (Corporate Social Responsibility) is not allowed as business expenditure under S.37(1).

column3: Chapter IV - Profits and Gains of Business or Profession
column5: Disallowance of Cash Payments above 10,000
column6: Where the assessee incurs any expenditure in respect of which a payment or aggregate of payments made to a person in a day, otherwise than by an account payee cheque or account payee bank draft or electronic clearing system, exceeds ten thousand rupees, no deduction shall be allowed.
column7: Section 40A(3) disallows tax deductions for business expenses if the payment is made in cash and exceeds ₹10,000 to a single person in a single day. The entire expense is disallowed and added back to taxable profits. For payments made to transport operators (hiring/leasing goods carriages), the cash limit is higher, at ₹35,000.
column8: Section 40A(3), Cash payment disallowance, cash limit 10000, transporter cash limit 35000, business cash payments, S.40A(3)
column9: Attar Singh Gurmukh Singh v. CIT
column11: Supreme Court upheld the validity of S.40A(3); it is designed to check tax evasion, ensure cash flow transparency, and verify transaction genuineness.
column20: 100% of the expenditure is disallowed if paid in cash above ₹10,000
column21: Exceptions listed under Rule 6DD (e.g. payments to RBI, banks, government, cultivators for agricultural produce, or where banking facilities are unavailable).

column3: Chapter IV - Profits and Gains of Business or Profession
column5: Deductions Allowed only on Actual Payment (including MSME S.43B(h))
column6: Notwithstanding anything contained in any other provision of this Act, a deduction otherwise allowable under this Act in respect of any sum payable... shall be allowed only in computing the income of the previous year in which such sum is actually paid.
column7: Section 43B lists expenses that are allowed as deductions only in the financial year they are actually paid, rather than when they are accrued. This includes taxes, duties, provident fund/ESI contributions, interest on bank loans, and leave encashment. Crucially, Section 43B(h) (introduced from FY 2023-24) mandates that payments to registered Micro and Small Enterprises (MSMEs) must be made within the time limit specified in the MSMED Act, 2006 (within 15 days, or max 45 days if there is a written agreement). If not paid within this limit, the deduction is disallowed in that FY and allowed only in the year of payment.
column8: Section 43B, Actual payment deduction, MSME 45 days payment rule, Section 43B(h) MSME, delayed payment to small business, disallowance
column9: Allied Motors Pvt. Ltd. v. CIT
column11: Established that if sales tax/duty is paid before the due date of filing the income tax return, the deduction under S.43B is allowed in the year of accrual.
column20: Disallowed if unpaid within due dates
column21: S.43B(h) does not apply to Medium enterprises (only Micro and Small) and does not apply to traders.

column3: Chapter VI - Set-off, or Carry Forward and Set-off of Loss
column5: Unexplained Cash Credits (Bank Deposits and Loans)
column6: Where any sum is found credited in the books of an assessee maintained for any previous year, and the assessee offers no explanation about the nature and source thereof or the explanation offered is not satisfactory, the sum so credited may be charged to income-tax.
column7: Section 68 applies when money is credited in the taxpayer's books of accounts (including unexplained bank deposits, share capital, unsecured loans, or gifts) and the taxpayer cannot satisfactorily explain its source. The Assessing Officer can treat this unexplained amount as taxable income. Under Section 115BBE, such unexplained income is taxed at a flat rate of 60% plus 25% surcharge and 4% cess, bringing the effective tax rate to 78%, with no deductions or basic exemption allowed.
column8: Section 68, Unexplained cash credit, unexplained bank deposits, loan from friend tax, S.68, Section 115BBE, 78 percent tax
column11: If the assessee provides the names, addresses, and PANs of the creditors/depositors, the burden shifts to the Revenue; it cannot make additions under S.68 without verification.
column12: NRA Iron & Steel Pvt. Ltd. v. CIT
column14: Supreme Court held that the taxpayer must prove: 1. Identity of the creditor; 2. Creditworthiness of the creditor; 3. Genuineness of the transaction, especially for share premium/loans.
column20: Taxed at 78% flat rate under Section 115BBE
column21: No basic exemption limit or business loss set-off can be claimed against S.68 income.

column3: Chapter VI - Set-off, or Carry Forward and Set-off of Loss
column5: Unexplained Investments and Money
column6: Where in the financial year immediately preceding the assessment year, the assessee has made investments which are not recorded in the books of account, and the assessee offers no explanation, the value of the investments may be deemed to be the income.
column7: Section 69 (along with S.69A, 69B, 69C) covers unexplained investments (buying property, gold, shares), unexplained cash, jewelry, or unexplained expenditures not recorded in the books. If the taxpayer cannot prove the source of funds, it is treated as deemed income and taxed at the punitive rate of 78% (60% tax + 25% surcharge + cess) under Section 115BBE, and penalties can also be levied.
column8: Section 69, Unexplained investment, S.69A unexplained money, property purchase cash source, S.115BBE, cash search seizure
column11: The word 'may' in S.69 gives discretion to the Assessing Officer; if the taxpayer has no source of income (e.g. a young student) and the investment is small, it need not automatically be added as income.
column20: Taxed at 78% flat rate under Section 115BBE
column21: Penalty of 10% under S.271AAC if unexplained income is voluntarily declared in ITR; otherwise 60% penalty if detected during audit.

column3: Chapter IV - Computation of Total Income
column5: Capital Gains Exemption on Sale of Residential House
column6: Where the capital gain arises from the transfer of a long-term capital asset, being a residential house, and the assessee has within a period of one year before or two years after purchased, or within three years constructed, a residential house, the gain shall be exempt.
column7: Section 54 provides an exemption on Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG) arising from the sale of a residential house. To claim the exemption, the gains must be reinvested in another residential house in India. The new house must be purchased within 1 year before or 2 years after the sale, or constructed within 3 years. From FY 2023-24, the maximum capital gains exemption under S.54 is capped at ₹10 Crore. If the money is not fully utilized before the ITR filing due date, it must be deposited in the Capital Gains Accounts Scheme (CGAS).
column8: Section 54, Capital gains house sale, capital gains account scheme, CGAS, reinvestment home, LTCG exemption property, S.54
column9: CIT v. Podar Cement Pvt. Ltd.
column11: Established that actual control and beneficial ownership is key, even if registration of the new property is pending.
column20: 100% of capital gains up to a maximum cap of ₹10 Crore
column21: The new property must not be sold within 3 years from its purchase/construction date; otherwise, the exempted gain is deducted from its cost price when computing tax on the new sale.

column3: Chapter IV - Computation of Total Income
column5: Capital Gains Exemption via S.54EC Bonds
column6: Where the capital gain arises from the transfer of a long-term capital asset, being land or building or both, and the assessee has within a period of six months invested the whole or any part of capital gains in the long-term specified asset.
column7: Section 54EC offers a way to save tax on Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG) arising from the sale of land or building by investing the gains in specified NHAI, REC, PFC, or IRFC bonds. The investment must be made within 6 months of the sale date. The bonds have a lock-in period of 5 years. The maximum investment limit is ₹50 Lakhs per financial year.
column8: Section 54EC, Capital gains bonds, NHAI bonds capital gains, REC bonds saving tax, 5 year lock-in property sale, S.54EC
column20: Maximum investment of ₹50 Lakhs (tax exemption matching amount invested)
column21: Bonds cannot be transferred, sold, or pledged as security for a loan within 5 years; doing so makes the exempted gain taxable.

column3: Chapter IV - Computation of Total Income
column5: Capital Gains Exemption on Sale of Other Assets (Shares, Gold etc.)
column6: Where the capital gain arises from the transfer of any long-term capital asset, not being a residential house, and the assessee has within a period of one year before or two years after purchased, or within three years constructed, a residential house.
column7: Section 54F provides an exemption on Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG) from the sale of any asset *other than* a residential house (such as equity shares, mutual funds, gold, commercial property). To claim this exemption, the entire *net sale consideration* (not just the capital gain) must be reinvested in purchasing or constructing one residential house in India. The purchase/construction timelines are the same as S.54. The maximum exemption is capped at ₹10 Crore, and the taxpayer must not own more than one residential house on the date of transfer.
column8: Section 54F, Save tax on gold sale, capital gains shares property, reinvest net consideration house, S.54F, gold capital gains
column20: Proportionate exemption based on net consideration reinvested, capped at ₹10 Crore
column21: The taxpayer must not buy another house within 2 years or construct another within 3 years of the transfer date.

column3: Chapter XX-B - Requirement as to Mode of Acceptance, Payment or Repayment in Certain Cases
column5: Restrictions on Accepting Cash Loans and Deposits above 20,000
column6: No person shall take or accept from any other person, any loan or deposit or any specified sum, otherwise than by an account payee cheque or account payee bank draft or use of electronic clearing system, if the amount is twenty thousand rupees or more.
column7: Section 269SS prohibits accepting loans, deposits, or advances for transfer of immovable property in cash if the total amount is ₹20,000 or more. Any transaction of ₹20,000 or above must be done through banking channels (cheque, draft, net banking, UPI, cards). If violated, a penalty equal to 100% of the loan amount is levied under S.271D.
column8: Section 269SS, Cash loan limit, cash deposit 20000, friend loan cash penalty, Section 271D penalty, cash property advance, S.269SS
column9: Assistant Director of Inspection (Investigation) v. Kum. A.B. Shanthi
column11: Supreme Court upheld the constitutionality of S.269SS. It was enacted to prevent tax evaders from explaining away unaccounted cash discovered during search operations as loans from friends.
column20: 100% penalty on the amount accepted in cash
column21: Does not apply if both the giver and receiver have agricultural income and no other taxable income.

column3: Chapter XX-B - Requirement as to Mode of Acceptance, Payment or Repayment in Certain Cases
column5: Restrictions on Repaying Loans and Deposits in Cash
column6: No branch of a banking company or a co-operative bank and no other person shall repay any loan or deposit made with it or any specified sum otherwise than by an account payee cheque or account payee bank draft, if the amount is twenty thousand rupees or more.
column7: Section 269T prohibits repaying any loan, deposit, or property advance in cash if the amount is ₹20,000 or more. If a person repays ₹20,000 or more in cash, they face a 100% penalty of the repaid amount under Section 271E.
column8: Section 269T, Cash loan repayment limit, repaying cash penalty, cash bank deposit withdrawal, Section 271E penalty, S.269T
column20: 100% penalty on the amount repaid in cash under Section 271E
column21: Does not apply if the loan is repaid to the government, banking company, or government corporations.

column3: Chapter II - Incomes which do not form part of Total Income
column5: Taxability of Life Insurance Policy Maturity Payouts
column6: Any sum received under a life insurance policy, including the sum allocated by way of bonus on such policy, is exempt from tax, subject to certain conditions.
column7: Section 10(10D) provides that maturity proceeds of a life insurance policy are tax-free, EXCEPT in these cases: 1. If premium in any year exceeds 10% of sum assured (for policies bought after April 2012; 20% for older policies). 2. For ULIPs (Unit Linked Insurance Plans) bought after Feb 2021, if annual premium exceeds ₹2.5 Lakhs. 3. For traditional plans bought after 1 April 2023, if aggregate annual premium exceeds ₹5 Lakhs. In case of the policyholder's death, the entire death benefit remains fully tax-free regardless of premium amounts.
column8: Section 10(10D), Life insurance tax maturity, LIC tax free proceeds, sum assured 10 percent, traditional plan 5 lakh premium, ULIP tax limit, S.10(10D)
column20: Fully exempt unless premium limits are breached
column21: Death benefits are always tax-free without any threshold limit.

column3: Chapter IV - Computation of Total Income
column5: Taxation of Gifts Received by Individuals
column6: Where any person receives, in any previous year, from any person or persons on or after the 1st day of April, 2017 - (a) any sum of money, without consideration, the aggregate value of which exceeds fifty thousand rupees.
column7: Section 56(2)(x) governs tax on gifts (cash, property, jewelry, shares). If aggregate value of gifts from non-relatives exceeds ₹50,000 in a year, the entire value is taxed as 'Income from Other Sources' under slabs. Exemptions (no tax at all): 1. Gifts from relatives (spouse, parents, siblings, children, lineage ancestors/descendants). 2. Gifts received on marriage. 3. Gifts received under a will or inheritance. 4. Gifts from local authorities, registered trusts.
column8: Section 56(2)(x), Gift tax limit, cash gift relative tax exemption, marriage gifts tax, property gift from friend, S.56(2)(x)
column11: Established that gift transactions between non-relatives must show valid love and affection to be genuine, otherwise taxed.
column20: Taxed under standard individual slabs if total exceeds ₹50,000
column21: Gifts from defined relatives are fully exempt without any upper limit.

column3: Chapter IV - Computation of Total Income
column5: Capital Gains Stamp Duty Valuation (Circle Rate)
column6: Where the consideration received or accruing as a result of the transfer of a capital asset, being land or building or both, is less than the value adopted by any authority of a State Government for the purpose of payment of stamp duty, the value so adopted shall be deemed to be the full value.
column7: Section 50C applies to sale of land or building. If the declared sale price is lower than the State Government's circle rate (stamp duty value), the circle rate is treated as the sale price to compute capital gains tax. However, a safe harbor limit of 10% is allowed: if the stamp duty value does not exceed 110% of the actual transaction price, the actual transaction price is accepted as the sale price.
column8: Section 50C, Stamp duty value capital gains, circle rate sale price tax, 110 percent safe harbor property, S.50C
column9: CIT v. Reliance Petroproducts Pvt. Ltd.
column11: Valuations by government authorities are presumed correct unless disputed before valuation officers.
column20: Deemed sale consideration matches stamp duty value if transaction is undervalued
column21: Allows taxpayer to request reference to a Valuation Officer if they claim actual market value is lower than circle rate.

column3: Chapter XVII - Collection and Recovery of Tax
column5: TDS on Sale of Immovable Property
column6: Any person, being a transferee, responsible for paying to a resident transferor any sum by way of consideration for transfer of any immovable property shall, at the time of credit or payment, deduct an amount equal to one per cent of such sum as income-tax.
column7: Section 194-IA mandates that the buyer of immovable property (land or building, other than agricultural land) costing ₹50 Lakhs or more must deduct 1% TDS from the sale price before paying the seller. The buyer must deposit this TDS using Form 26QB using the seller's PAN. Seller can claim credit for this TDS during filing. If the seller does not provide a PAN, the TDS rate increases to 20%.
column8: Section 194-IA, TDS on property purchase 50 Lakhs, Form 26QB property TDS, 1 percent TDS land house, S.194-IA
column20: 1% of total transaction value (20% if no PAN provided)
column21: Does not apply to agricultural land.

column3: Chapter XVII - Collection and Recovery of Tax
column5: TDS on Rent Paid by Individuals and HUFs
column6: Any person, being an individual or a Hindu undivided family (other than those referred to in S.194I), responsible for paying to a resident any income by way of rent exceeding fifty thousand rupees for a month shall deduct five per cent.
column7: Section 194-IB mandates that individuals or HUFs who pay rent exceeding ₹50,000 per month for residential or commercial property must deduct 5% TDS from the rent. The TDS must be deducted once a year (at the end of the FY or when vacating the property) and deposited using Form 26QC. This TDS does not require a TAN (Tax Deduction Account Number); the buyer can use their PAN.
column8: Section 194-IB, TDS on rent above 50000, Form 26QC rent TDS, individual pay rent TDS, S.194-IB
column20: 5% of total rent paid in a financial year (20% if no PAN provided)
column21: TAN is not required; payment and return are combined in Form 26QC.

column3: Chapter XVII - Collection and Recovery of Tax
column5: TCS on Foreign Remittances (LRS) and Tour Packages
column6: Every person, being an authorised dealer, who receives an amount, for remittance out of India from a buyer, being a person remitting such amount under the Liberalised Remittance Scheme shall collect TCS.
column7: Section 206C(1G) governs Tax Collected at Source (TCS) on foreign remittances under RBI's Liberalised Remittance Scheme (LRS) and overseas tour packages. Effective from 1 October 2023: 1. Overseas Tour Packages: 5% TCS up to ₹7 Lakhs, and 20% TCS on amounts above ₹7 Lakhs. 2. Other Remittances (except Education/Medical): Nil up to ₹7 Lakhs, and 20% TCS above ₹7 Lakhs. 3. Education Loans: 0.5% TCS above ₹7 Lakhs.
column8: Section 206C(1G), TCS on foreign travel, LRS foreign remittance TCS, 20 percent TCS, sending money abroad tax, S.206C(1G)
column20: 5% or 20% TCS depending on purpose and limit
column21: TCS is not an additional tax; it can be claimed as a credit or refund while filing the annual income tax return.

column3: Chapter IV - Profits and Gains of Business or Profession
column5: Presumptive Taxation for Goods Transporters
column6: Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in sections 28 to 43C, in the case of an assessee, who owns not more than ten goods carriages and who is engaged in the business of plying, hiring or leasing.
column7: Section 44AE provides a presumptive tax scheme for small goods transporters owning 10 or fewer trucks/goods carriages. The taxable income is calculated on a flat rate basis: 1. Heavy Goods Vehicle (capacity > 12,000 kg): ₹1,000 per metric ton of gross vehicle weight for every month or part of a month. 2. Other Vehicles: ₹7,500 per month or part of a month. Genuinely no need to maintain books of accounts or undergo tax audit.
column8: Section 44AE, Transporter presumptive tax, truck owner tax scheme, 7500 per month vehicle, S.44AE
column19: Profits and Gains of Business or Profession
column20: Fixed presumptive income per month of vehicle ownership
column21: Assessee must own 10 or fewer vehicles during the entire previous year.

column3: Chapter XIV - Procedure for Assessment
column5: Mandatory PAN-Aadhaar Linking
column6: Every person who has been allotted a permanent account number as on the 1st day of July, 2017, and who is eligible to obtain Aadhaar number, shall intimate his Aadhaar number.
column7: Section 139AA makes it mandatory to link PAN (Permanent Account Number) with Aadhaar. If a taxpayer fails to link them by the government's deadline (which was 30 June 2023), the PAN becomes 'inoperative'. Consequences of an inoperative PAN: 1. Taxpayer cannot file ITR; 2. Pending refunds are not processed; 3. TDS is deducted at double/punitive rates (usually 20%). A late fee of ₹1,000 must be paid to link the PAN-Aadhaar.
column8: Section 139AA, PAN Aadhaar linking mandatory, inoperative PAN consequences, link PAN fee 1000, double TDS PAN, S.139AA
column20: PAN becomes inoperative if not linked; fee of ₹1,000 to reactivate
column21: Exemptions: NRI, non-citizens, individuals aged 80+ years, and residents of Assam, Jammu & Kashmir, and Meghalaya.

column3: Chapter VI - Set-off, or Carry Forward and Set-off of Loss
column5: Set-off and Carry Forward of Losses
column6: Where in respect of any assessment year the net result of the computation under any head of income... is a loss, the assessee shall be entitled to have the amount of such loss set off.
column7: Section 71 & 72 govern how losses can be offset against other income. 1. House Property Loss: Can be set off against any other income up to ₹2,00,000 per year; excess can be carried forward for 8 years (and set off only against house property income). 2. Business Loss: Cannot be set off against Salary income; can carry forward for 8 years. 3. Capital Losses: Short-term capital loss can be set off against both STCG and LTCG; Long-term capital loss can *only* be set off against LTCG. Capital losses can carry forward for 8 years.
column8: Section 71, Business loss set off salary, house property loss limit 2 lakh, carry forward losses 8 years, short term capital loss set off, S.71, S.72
column9: CIT v. Harprasad & Co. Pvt. Ltd.
column11: Established that if an income is exempt from tax, the loss from that source cannot be set off against other taxable income.
column20: Strict set-off limits as specified per head of income
column21: Losses cannot be carried forward if the ITR is filed after the due date (except for house property loss).

column3: Chapter XVII - Collection and Recovery of Tax
column5: Late Filing Fee for Income Tax Return (ITR)
column6: Without prejudice to the provisions of this Act, where a person required to furnish a return of income under section 139, fails to do so within the time prescribed, he shall pay by way of fee.
column7: Section 234F mandates a late fee if the Income Tax Return (ITR) is filed after the statutory due date (usually 31 July for individuals). Slabs: 1. If total taxable income is ₹5 Lakhs or less: Late fee is ₹1,000. 2. If total taxable income exceeds ₹5 Lakhs: Late fee is ₹5,000. This fee must be paid before submitting the delayed return.
column8: Section 234F, ITR late fee 5000, late filing fee 1000, delayed tax return penalty, July 31 deadline penalty, S.234F
column20: ₹1,000 or ₹5,000 depending on taxable income
column21: No fee is payable if total gross income does not exceed the basic exemption limit.
